metadata.working_title: A Prefrontal Network Model Operating Near Steady and Oscillatory States Links Spike Desynchronization and Synaptic Deficits in Schizophrenia

abstract_sketch.problem: Schizophrenia involves functional disruption of prefrontal cortical networks, but the link between synaptic-level NMDAR deficits and network-level spike desynchronization is poorly understood.
abstract_sketch.gap: No prior model explains how NMDAR hypofunction prevents the steady-to-oscillatory transition in prefrontal circuits that gives rise to synchronous spiking during behavior.
abstract_sketch.approach: A recurrent spiking network (N=5,000 leaky integrate-and-fire neurons) operating near the boundary between asynchronous irregular and synchronous irregular regimes, analyzed via mean field and linear stability analysis.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Critical-state network transitions to ~50 Hz oscillation with emergent 0-lag synchrony when external input increases by 5%; reducing recurrent NMDAR currents prevents this transition; model reproduces experimental correlation peaks at +/- 20 ms lags (vs +/- 18 ms in data) and ~55 Hz gamma oscillation observed in monkey PFC during DPX task.
abstract_sketch.impact: Provides a circuit-level mechanism linking NMDAR synaptic deficits to disrupted spike timing, explaining how schizophrenia-associated NMDAR hypofunction could disconnect prefrontal circuits via spike-timing dependent plasticity.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Schizophrenia results in functional disruption of prefrontal cortical networks (Insel 2010; Jauhar 2022)
Parallel primate and mouse models converge on reduced 0-lag synchrony and reduced synaptic information transfer (Zick 2018, Kummerfeld 2020, Zick 2021)
Theoretical work establishes asynchronous irregular and synchronous irregular regimes in balanced networks (Brunel 2000; van Vreeswijk & Sompolinsky 1996)
Gap: no prior work explains how NMDAR hypofunction desynchronizes PFC spiking via transitions between dynamical regimes
Contribution: spiking network model near the steady-oscillatory boundary explains NMDAR-dependent synchrony emergence
Link to spike-timing dependent plasticity and potential disease mechanism (Dan & Poo 2004; He 2015; Yagishita 2014)

subsections.title: Experimental Observations in Monkey PFC
content_bullets: DPX task structure: cue 1000 ms, delay 1000 ms, probe 500 ms
AX 69% of trials, non-AX (AY/BX/BY) 31%
Drug-naive neuron pairs: 978 (cue-aligned), 1246 (response-aligned); drug: 368, 434
Spike synchrony increases sharply ~200 ms before motor response; abolished by PCP (0.25-0.30 mg/kg IM)
Correlation peaks at +/- 18 ms lags, ~55 Hz gamma oscillation
Electrodes 400 um apart, 400-1400 um range

subsections.title: Network Model Architecture
content_bullets: N = 5,000 LIF neurons: NE = 4,000 excitatory (80%), NI = 1,000 inhibitory (20%)
Random connectivity, p = 0.2, average C = 10^3 connections per neuron
Four synaptic currents: AMPA, NMDA, GABA (recurrent), AMPA (external)
NMDAR voltage dependence: beta = 0.062 mV^-1, gamma = 3.57 mM, [Mg2+] = 1 mM
Neuron params: E (Cm=0.5 nF, gm=25 nS, tau_rp=2 ms), I (Cm=0.2 nF, gm=20 nS, tau_rp=1 ms)

subsections.title: Mean Field Analysis and Linear Stability
content_bullets: Mean field approach extended to conductance-based synapses (Brunel & Wang 2001; Renart 2003)
State diagram in (I_AMPA/I_GABA, I_X/I_theta) parameter plane
Critical line where instability rate lambda = 0 separates steady/oscillatory regimes
Oscillation frequency at onset: f_ntwrk = omega / 2pi

subsections.title: Primary Network Selection: Steady vs Critical States
content_bullets: Both configured with nu_X = 5 Hz, I_NMDA/I_GABA = 0.2, prescribed rates 5 Hz (E) and 20 Hz (I)
Steady state: deep within stable region, baseline 5.2/20 Hz (E/I)
Critical state: near oscillatory boundary, baseline 5.5/21 Hz (E/I)

subsections.title: Direct Simulations with 5% Input Perturbation
content_bullets: Steady state +5% input: modest rate increase to 7.8/26 Hz, still asynchronous
Critical state +5% input: qualitative transition to ~50 Hz oscillation, rates 15/42 Hz
Individual neurons still fire irregularly at rates << oscillation frequency
Theoretically predicted onset frequency: 58 Hz
Temporal correlations show peaks at +/- 20 ms lags

subsections.title: NMDAR Reduction Prevents Oscillatory Transition
content_bullets: Reducing recurrent NMDAR currents prevents critical network from transitioning to oscillatory state
Zero-lag synchrony fails to emerge with reduced NMDAR
Directly parallels experimental PCP abolition of pre-response synchrony

subsections.title: Direct Comparison with Experimental Data
content_bullets: Model ~50 Hz oscillation vs experimental ~55 Hz
Model correlation peaks +/- 20 ms vs experimental +/- 18 ms
Model predicted onset 58 Hz
Both show desynchronization with NMDAR block

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Proximity to steady-oscillatory boundary explains sensitivity to input and NMDAR
Reducing NMDAR prevents transition; mechanistic link to schizophrenia
Implications for spike-timing dependent plasticity and active disconnection hypothesis
Relation to prior work (Brunel 2000, Brunel & Wang 2003, Compte 2000)
Limitations: random connectivity, simplified LIF, no dopamine

sections.title: Materials and Methods
content_bullets: DPX task and electrode recording protocols
Spike synchrony metrics (2 ms resolution, 100 ms window)
Network model equations and parameters
Mean field approximation and linear stability analysis
Simulation protocol (dt = 0.1 ms)

van Vreeswijk Sompolinsky balanced network chaos
Zick 2018 blocking NMDAR prefrontal spike timing
must_cite: Zick et al 2018 Neuron (primary experimental data)
Brunel 2000 Journal of Computational Neuroscience (theoretical framework)
Brunel & Wang 2003 Journal of Neurophysiology (fast oscillations)
Jahr & Stevens 1990 Journal of Neuroscience (NMDAR kinetics)
